topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile finality of a closing vault. Subject line: Retirement Notification. The body was a curt, two-sentence statement confirming Dr. Alistair Finch’s departure from the institute. No farewell. No indication of where he was going, no mention of the years of guidance he’d poured into me. I reread the message three times, the words blurring slightly on the screen. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a familiar, unpleasant clenching sensation. I felt a sharp, involuntary need to slam my laptop shut, the click echoing too loudly in the quiet office. I stood up, my hands trembling just slightly, and walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape. The air felt thick, heavy, as if I couldn't draw a full breath.

[story 2]

When I finally saw him at the reception desk, the air seemed to thin out around me. Alistair was there, impeccably dressed, radiating a sort of detached calm that felt entirely alien. He offered a brief nod, a gesture devoid of warmth or recognition, and then turned to address a junior colleague, his voice smooth and professionally distant. My jaw felt tight, my mouth dry. I managed a stiff, almost painful smile that didn't reach my eyes. Every movement I made felt exaggerated, clumsy, as if I were performing a ritual I didn't understand. I watched him walk away, his silhouette receding into the hallway, and I remained rooted to the spot, my knuckles white where I gripped my briefcase. The silence that followed his departure was loud, pressing in on my ears.

[story 3]

I found the notice tucked inside a worn copy of a textbook, a careless placement that somehow amplified the sting. It was just a typed sheet, stark and unfeeling. No signature, no handwritten note, just the administrative announcement. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making the room feel suddenly too bright, too sharp. I picked up the paper, the edges crinkling under my fingers, and slowly, deliberately, I folded it into a tight, hard square. The paper felt flimsy, inadequate to contain the weight of the abrupt severance. I let the folded paper drop onto the desk with a soft thud, the sound strangely final. I didn't look back at the door, focusing instead on the intricate pattern of dust motes dancing in a shaft of sunlight—a pointless, futile attempt to find some order in the sudden, jarring emptiness.
